1:
The witnesses take this opening muster as a study in generalship under grief, and they agree on the facts while dividing over the numbers. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the setting: Charles Ellicott insists the verb means not merely 'numbered' but mustered, and pictures David some time at Mahanaim organising the forces that continually gathered to him there, so that the little band of his flight has grown into an army. Adam Clarke gives the overview — David reviews and arranges the people and commits the command to Joab, Abishai, and Ittai — and notes how greatly the small company had been recruited.

The one real dispute is the size of that army, and it turns on a single verse ahead. John Gill reports the figure from Josephus, about four thousand, but adds honestly that 'one would think there should be more by what follows.' Clarke records the alternative: some read the words of v.3 not as 'thou art worth ten thousand' but as 'we are now ten thousand strong,' which would swell the host considerably. The text leaves the count open, and the commentators are content to leave it so.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the muster into a lesson on the discipline of faith. Before David counted his forces, Henry observes, he had 'committed his cause to God by prayer, for that was his relief in all his afflictions,' and only then took an account of his men, dividing them into right wing, left wing, and centre under his two old experienced generals and his new friend Ittai. From this he draws the maxim that 'good order and good conduct may sometimes be as serviceable in an army as great numbers,' for wisdom teaches us to make the best of the strength we have and let it reach to the utmost.

Henry also anticipates the pathos that will govern the whole chapter. David's true friends would not let him go to the field, remembering the design to smite the king only; and Henry reads a providence in it — 'God's providence wisely ordered it, that David should not be in the field of battle; for then his tenderness would certainly have interposed to save the life of Absalom, whom God had determined to destroy.' Already the collision is set between a father's love and a nation's justice. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and the reformed Gill add nothing to quarrel over; they, with Henry and Ellicott, agree that the strength of David's cause lay less in his thousands than in the God to whom he had prayed, and that the good order of a small, well-led host would shortly prove more than a match for Absalom's unwieldy multitude.
2:
The verse records the ordering of the host into three divisions, and the witnesses treat it chiefly as a matter of military custom and of David's thwarted resolve to lead in person. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the background. Charles Ellicott notes that the arrangement of an army in three divisions was common both among the Israelites — he points to Gideon's companies (Judges 7:16), to Jephthah, and to Saul at Jabesh (1 Samuel 11:11) — and among their enemies, whose spoilers came out in three companies (1 Samuel 13:17); he compares too the later muster of the guard (2 Kings 11:5). The threefold division, then, is no innovation but the settled practice of the field, and David follows it, committing a third each to Joab, to Abishai the son of Zeruiah, and to Ittai the Gittite.

Keil and Delitzsch read the sequence closely: David sent forth the three parts, and then said to the people, 'I will surely go forth with you myself also' — the king's own proposal to take the chief command in person, which the next verse will overrule. They render the coming charge in their plain way, that in the hearing of all he commanded the principal generals, 'Mildly for me with the boy Absalom,' already sounding the note of tenderness. Ellicott agrees that David proposed to lead the army himself.

The reformed Gill adds the detail that the middle division under Joab was very likely the centre of the army, since Joab was the general-in-chief, and observes shrewdly that this distribution was arranged while David still intended to head the host himself, and so was framed with respect to that intention. The care over which commander held which wing is not idle: it prepares for the tragedy, since it is Joab, entrusted with the centre and with the king's express charge in his ears, who will disobey it.

There is no debate here, only a convergence of witnesses on the competence of the arrangement. What the commentators quietly mark is the pairing of two old warriors, Joab and Abishai, with the newcomer Ittai — the Gittite stranger who in the previous chapter had refused to leave David and now shares the command of a third of Israel's loyal army. The honour done to a foreigner and the trust reposed in the sons of Zeruiah frame the whole engagement; and over it all hangs the king's twofold desire, to fight beside his men and to spare the son who had risen against him. The first desire the people will deny him; the second, his generals.
3:
The people's refusal to let David take the field draws the witnesses into a small but genuine textual crux, and behind it a lesson on the worth of a king. The grammatical-historical and wesleyan-arminian commentators together press the emendation. Adam Clarke observes that the Hebrew particle rendered 'now' (attah) is doubtless a mistake for the pronoun 'thou' (attah), the two differing by a single letter, and that this is how the Septuagint, the Vulgate, and the Chaldee read it, supported by manuscripts of Kennicott and De Rossi. Charles Ellicott lays it out the same way: the received text, as it stands, must be translated 'now there are ten thousand like us,' but the change of one letter turns 'now' into 'thou,' and this change 'should unquestionably be made' in accordance with the ancient versions and the English. So the people's argument becomes the memorable tribute, 'thou art worth ten thousand of us.'

Ellicott draws out the reasoning the people urge. David is 'the very centre of their whole cause,' and they suggest that, even while avoiding unnecessary exposure, he may yet be equally helpful by keeping a reserve in the city to succour them in case of need. Their words weigh the arithmetic of the throne: were half the army to fall, the enemy would not much care; but the loss of the king would be the loss of everything. It is the same estimate David's true friends had made in v.1, that the war was aimed at the king's person.

The reformed Gill catches the tone of the exchange with a single stroke: 'they were as resolute as David.' The king had pressed his desire to venture with them, and the people press back with equal firmness — a loyal contest of wills in which subjects overrule their sovereign for his own good and the kingdom's.

The alternative reading noted under v.1 resurfaces here, for those who take the clause as 'we are now ten thousand strong' find in it a clue to the size of David's army; but the weight of the witnesses falls on the personal tribute, and with it on the doctrine that a righteous ruler must be valued by his people above their own safety, and must sometimes be willing, for their sakes, to be kept from danger rather than thrust into it. The verse thus honours both parties: the king ready to risk himself with his men, and the men wiser than their king in refusing the risk. Where David would spend himself, they will spend themselves for him, and the providence already noted quietly uses their loyalty to keep the tender father off the field where his heart would have betrayed his duty.
4:
David's yielding — 'What seemeth you best I will do' — the witnesses read as a rare and gracious flexibility in a king, and as the providential hinge that keeps him from the battlefield. The reformed Gill calls it 'an instance of great condescension in him,' and adds that it was his wisdom and prudence to yield at such a time, 'especially as their sentiments were founded on affection and loyalty to him.' The submission is not weakness but the discernment to be overruled by good counsel, even from inferiors, when their counsel plainly serves his interest.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads David's compliance with a shrewd eye to the king's own heart: 'David was nothing loth to avoid the personal encounter with his son, and readily yielded.' He did not, however, sink into idleness. He encouraged the troops by reviewing them as they passed out, and — Ellicott stresses — 'improved the opportunity to give his generals special and public charge concerning Absalom.' The very moment of standing aside becomes the moment of the charge that governs everything to come. Ellicott hears in David's chosen word a father's plea: he speaks of Absalom tenderly as 'the young man' (a phrase he will repeat in v.29 and v.32), 'to imply that his sin was a youthful indiscretion' — as if to soften into rashness what was in truth a settled rebellion.

The puritan-evangelical reading, carried by Matthew Henry from his comment on the opening verses, presses the providence. It was 'no piece of wisdom to be stiff in our resolutions,' Henry writes, but to be willing to hear reason and be overruled by advice when it appears for our own good; and whether or not the people's prudence had an eye to it, 'God's providence wisely ordered it, that David should not be in the field of battle; for then his tenderness would certainly have interposed to save the life of Absalom, whom God had determined to destroy.' The king's gentle acquiescence and God's hidden purpose run together in one act.

The witnesses do not divide. They agree that David's willingness to be gainsaid is itself a mark of his greatness — 'we must never reckon it an affront to be gain-said for our good,' as Henry puts it — and that the same humility which bows to his people's counsel now bends, too tenderly, toward his rebel son. The verse stands at the still point before the storm: David reviewing his loyal thousands at the gate, giving his commanders their orders, and unknowingly being kept back by heaven from the one scene where his love would have undone the justice about to fall. What the king yields to men, Providence turns to its own ends.
5:
The charge to 'deal gently for my sake with the young man, even with Absalom' is the emotional centre of the chapter, and the witnesses gather around it to weigh a father's love, a king's duty, and a type of divine mercy. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew with blunt tenderness — 'Mildly for me with the boy Absalom' — and note that all the people heard the king give this charge to all the captains, so that the coming disobedience is public and inexcusable.

The wesleyan-arminian voices probe David's motives most fully. Adam Clarke simply asks who, being a father with any natural feeling, would have acted otherwise: 'David was the father of this worthless young man; and is it to be wondered at that he feels as a father?' John Wesley unfolds the charge — if you conquer, take him prisoner but do not kill him — and traces it to four springs: David's great indulgence toward his children; his consciousness that he himself, by his own sin, was the meritorious cause of this rebellion, Absalom being given up to it for the punishment of David's sins; the consideration of Absalom's youth, which makes men foolish and open to ill counsel; and David's piety, 'being loth that he should be cut off in the act of his sin without any space for repentance.'

The puritan-evangelical Henry lifts the verse to its highest register, drawing in Bishop Joseph Hall's famous descant. 'What means this ill-placed love? This unjust mercy? Deal gently with a traitor? Of all traitors, with a son? Of all sons, with an Absalom?' — Hall marvels that the very cause of the quarrel, David's crown and blood, should become the motive of mercy, and grants that 'even in the holiest parents, nature may be guilty of an injurious tenderness, of a bloody indulgence.' Yet Hall and Henry both turn the excess into a figure: 'was not this done in type of that immeasurable mercy of the true King and Redeemer of Israel, who prayed for his persecutors, for his murderers, Father, forgive them?' Henry sets the two hearts side by side — 'Absalom would have David only smitten. David would have Absalom only spared' — and finds in the contrast a resemblance of man's wickedness toward God and God's mercy toward man, 'of which it is hard to say which is more amazing.'

The witnesses do not so much debate as layer their readings. Where Clarke and Wesley excuse or explain the father's weakness, Henry and Hall confess it a fault yet baptize it into a shadow of the Cross. All land on the same pastoral comfort Henry draws: when God sends an affliction to correct his children, it is with this charge, 'Deal gently with them for my sake; for he knows our frame.' The command that will be trampled in the wood becomes, in the witnesses' hands, a window into the mercy of the God who spares.
6:
The battle joins 'in the wood of Ephraim,' and the witnesses spend their energy on a stubborn geographical puzzle: how a wood bearing Ephraim's name could lie east of the Jordan, where the fighting plainly occurred. The grammatical-historical commentators carry the whole discussion, and here they genuinely differ. Charles Ellicott concedes that no wood of Ephraim on the eastern side of the Jordan is elsewhere named in Scripture, yet argues the battle must have been on that side for a chain of reasons: both armies were already there with no mention of crossing; David remained in Mahanaim with the reserves to succour the army; he received the news of Absalom's death there; the army returned thither after the battle (2 Samuel 19:3); and David had afterward to cross the Jordan on his final return to Jerusalem. 'There is really no difficulty,' Ellicott concludes, 'but such as arises from our ignorance of local names.'

Keil and Delitzsch take the other road. Both the name and the fact that, according to Joshua 17:15, the tribe-land of Ephraim abounded in forests favour the idea that this was a wood in the inheritance of Ephraim, on the western side of the Jordan; and they think this in harmony with the later notice that Ahimaaz took 'the way of the Jordan valley' to bring the news (v.23). The two schools of grammatical reading thus divide cleanly: Ellicott placing the wood east of the river against its name, Keil and Delitzsch placing it west with its name — a debate the text does not finally settle, since, as Ellicott admits, the local names of that age are largely lost to us.

The reformed Gill contributes the vivid detail from Josephus, who called it 'a great field, with a wood behind it' — a plain fit for the clash of armies backed by a forest fit for the rout that followed. That simple picture already forecasts the strategy: David's smaller, disciplined host would meet Absalom's multitude in the open and then drive the broken enemy into the trees, where the ground itself would finish the work.

Beneath the topographical dispute the witnesses agree on the theology of the place. Henry, from his survey of the chapter, had noted that David deliberately met the enemy at some distance, 'lest he should bring that city into trouble which had so kindly sheltered him' — the loyalty of Mahanaim repaid by keeping the war from its walls. And all of them see in the choice of a wooded battlefield the hidden hand that will make the terrain an instrument of judgment. The name of Ephraim, wherever the wood stood, carries an old echo of tribal strife; the ground where Israel now falls before the servants of David is one more field where a divided people reaps the bitterness of rebellion.
7:
The rout of Absalom's host and the slaughter of twenty thousand the witnesses read as both a military fact and a stroke of divine justice. The grammatical-historical commentators handle the number with care. Charles Ellicott allows that twenty thousand 'seems large,' but reminds us we know nothing of the size of the forces engaged, and that if the phrase 'that day' be taken, as often, broadly enough to include the whole campaign of which this battle was the culmination, 'there is nothing surprising in the destruction of 20,000 men.' Adam Clarke raises the same caution from another angle: whether these fell on the field of battle or are reckoned together with those the wood devoured, 'we know not.' Keil and Delitzsch add that Israel — meaning the followers of Absalom — was smitten before David's warriors and 'sustained a loss of 20,000 men,' the conflict scattering over the whole face of that region.

Ellicott is candid that of the human causes of the victory nothing is told. He ventures a reasonable guess — that the advantage of thorough military organisation and generalship lay on David's side — but then names what he takes to be the deeper cause: 'the vast power of the right, the prestige of law and authority.' Absalom's cause was a usurpation, and a usurpation carries within it the seeds of its own collapse when it meets the settled order it has defied.

The reformed Gill states the plain sense — that 'the people of Israel that were under Absalom' were beaten by David's army — and lets the horror of a civil slaughter stand unglossed: these twenty thousand were Israelites, David's own subjects, cut down in a war they should never have waged.

The puritan-evangelical Henry presses that horror into a sermon on rebellion's wages. The rebels, he writes, 'smarted justly for their treason against their lawful prince, their uneasiness under so good a government, and their base ingratitude to so good a governor'; they found what it was to take up arms for a usurper who 'with his kisses and caresses had wheedled them into their own ruin.' Where now, Henry asks, are the rewards and golden days they had promised themselves? Now they see what it is 'to take counsel against the Lord and his anointed, and to think of breaking his bands asunder' — an echo of the second Psalm laid over the wood of Ephraim. Yet Henry keeps the tragedy double-edged: God 'herein fought for David, and yet fought against him; for all these that were slain were his own subjects, and the common interest of his kingdom was weakened by the slaughter.' The victory is real, the vindication is just, and still it is a wound in Israel's own body — which is why, he notes, the Romans allowed no triumph for a victory won in a civil war.
8:
'The wood devoured more people that day than the sword devoured' — this strange clause draws the witnesses into their liveliest debate of the chapter: by what agency did the forest kill? The wesleyan-arminian commentators lay out the options most fully. Adam Clarke reports the common supposition, that when the army was broken the fugitives betook themselves to the wood, fell into pits and swamps, and, being entangled, were hewn down by David's men; but he sets against it the ancient versions — 'the Chaldee, Syriac, and Arabic, state that they were devoured by wild beasts in the wood.' John Wesley gathers every possibility into one heap: more died in the wood through hunger, thirst, and weariness; or by the wild beasts, great numbers of which were there; or by falling into ditches and pits, probably covered with grass so that men could not see them till they fell; and especially by David's men, who pursued and killed them. The wood, Wesley concludes, is rightly said to have devoured them 'because it gave the occasion to their destruction,' its trees and pits entangling the fugitives and making them easy prey, while 'the sword' stands by a common figure for the battle in the open field.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the terrain that makes such carnage credible: the woody region was 'most likely full of ravines, precipices, and marshes, into which the flying foe was pursued, and where so many perished.' John Gill, the reformed voice, describes the panic itself — Absalom's soldiers, their ranks broken and thrown into utmost confusion, 'ran about here and there all over the field,' scattering into the neighbouring wood where the ground closed on them.

The debate, then, is real but bounded: whether the wood's devouring means chiefly the wild beasts of the ancient versions, or the natural hazards of pit and precipice, or the pursuing sword that overtook the entangled — most of the witnesses holding all three at once, with only the weight of emphasis differing. On the fact they are agreed: the terrain was David's silent ally, and the rout cost the rebels more than the battle.

The puritan-evangelical Henry reads the theology the others leave implicit. 'By that flight with which they hoped to save themselves they destroyed themselves,' he writes; the wood they sought for shelter devoured them, that they might learn how, when they thought themselves safe and said 'the bitterness of death is past,' the justice of God pursued them still. 'What refuge can rebels find from divine vengeance?' The very forest that promised escape became the executioner, so that heaven fought for David not only by the arm of his soldiers but by the roots and ravines of the ground — a judgment the more terrible for wearing no visible sword.
9:
Absalom caught fast in the great oak becomes for the witnesses the chapter's most memorable emblem, and they divide sharply over one detail: was he held by his hair or by his neck? The wesleyan-arminian and puritan-evangelical voices favour the hair, and turn it into a parable of pride. John Wesley supposes he was 'entangled by the hair of the head, which being very long and thick, might easily catch hold of a bough, especially when the great God directed it,' and adds that either he wore no helmet or had thrown it away to speed his flight: 'thus the matter of his pride was the instrument of his ruin.' Matthew Henry agrees that the twisted branches caught his head, 'either by his neck, or, as some think, by his long hair, which had been so much his pride, and was now justly made a halter for him.'

The grammatical-historical commentators are skeptical of the popular picture. Charles Ellicott states flatly that 'there is nothing said to support the common idea (which seems to have originated with Josephus), that he hung by his long hair,' though he grants the hair may have helped to entangle his head; the text says only that his head caught between the branches while the mule went from under him. Adam Clarke is blunter: 'It has been supposed that Absalom was caught by the hair, but no such thing is intimated in the text'; probably his neck was caught in the fork of a strong bough, and he was nearly dead when Joab found him, since the later phrase 'yet alive' hints that he was almost gone. Keil and Delitzsch steer a middle course: it is 'not clearly stated in the words that Absalom hung only by his hair,' but simply that his head was fastened in the terebinth, jammed between the strong boughs.

Beneath the dispute the witnesses converge on the theology of the scene, and here Henry soars. Absalom hung 'between heaven and earth, as unworthy of either, as abandoned of both; earth would not keep him, heaven would not take him.' His mule went out from under him 'as if glad to get clear of such a burden.' This was no common fall: had the mule merely thrown him, the same end would have followed, 'but that would have been too common a fate for so uncommon a criminal,' and so God, as with Dathan and Abiram (Numbers 16:30), 'create[d] a new thing.' Absalom is 'hung up, in terrorem — to frighten children from disobedience to their parents' (Proverbs 30:17). The reformed Gill notes the irony that Absalom, fleeing in terror, 'fled in the way of' David's men, who yet let him pass, remembering the king's charge. The rebel who began the fight expecting to triumph over his father hangs helpless, unable to fight or fly, the mark of his vanity turned into his gallows.
10:
A certain man sees Absalom hanging and reports it to Joab, and the witnesses read the moment for its timing and its restraint. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out the exchange: 'a man (one of David's men) saw him in this situation, and told Joab,' whereupon Joab answered, 'Behold, thou hast seen it, and wherefore hast thou not smitten him there to the ground? and it was for me to give thee ten silverlings and a girdle' — that is, had the soldier slain Absalom, it would have been Joab's duty to reward him. The reported speech already frames the collision to come between the king's charge and the general's will.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke fastens on the interval of time the verse implies, and reasons from it toward mercy. Absalom 'must have hung there a considerable time': the man saw him hanging, though how long he had hung before being seen we cannot tell; the man then came and informed Joab, which took further time; Joab then went and pierced him with three darts, which took more time still. 'It is therefore natural to conclude,' Clarke writes, 'that his life must have been nearly gone after having been so long suspended, and probably was past recovery, even if Joab had taken him down.' The observation is not idle: it softens, though it cannot excuse, what Joab is about to do, by suggesting that the hanging itself had already all but killed the rebel prince.

The reformed Gill marks simply that the man was 'one of David's soldiers' who saw him 'in the above posture' — the helpless suspension described in the previous verse. The plainness of the notice is itself telling: the whole loyal army had been charged, in the hearing of all, to spare the young man, and so the soldier who finds him does exactly what the charge required — he does not strike, but reports.

The witnesses do not divide here; they let the scene do its work. Matthew Henry, surveying the passage, sees Absalom now 'set up for a spectacle, as well as a mark, that the righteous might see him and laugh at him' (Psalms 52:6), and adds the sting that of all the friends he had courted and confided in, 'though he hung long enough to have been relieved, yet he had none at hand to disentangle him.' The soldier's obedience to David's word thus stands in silent contrast to the disobedience Joab will show: the common man keeps the king's charge; the great commander will break it. Between the seeing and the slaying lies the whole moral distance of the chapter — the space in which one man remembers the king's mercy and another prepares to override it.
11:
Joab's rebuke of the soldier — why did you not strike him down, that I might have rewarded you? — the witnesses read as the first open breach with David's charge, and they linger over the promised reward. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the sense: 'wherefore hast thou not smitten him there to the ground? and I would have given thee ten shekels of silver, and a girdle' — Joab plainly declaring that the killing of Absalom would have earned a bounty, in flat contradiction of the king's public command that none touch the young man.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke enriches the scene with the lore of the girdle. 'The military belt was the chief ornament of a soldier, and was highly prized in all ancient nations; it was also a rich present from one chieftain to another.' He recalls that Jonathan gave his girdle to David as 'the highest pledge of his esteem and perpetual friendship' (1 Samuel 18:4), and that in Homer Ajax gave his to Hector as a token of the highest respect. The girdle, then, was no trifle thrown in with the silver but a mark of honour and advancement — Matthew Henry takes the whole reward to signify 'a captain's commission.' Joab is offering promotion for the deed the king had forbidden.

The reformed Gill notes only that Joab 'said unto the man that told him,' the man 'that gave the above account of him' — keeping the thread taut between the report and the reproof. The economy is deliberate: Gill lets Joab's own words expose him, without editorial comment.

The witnesses agree, without needing to argue, on what the verse reveals. Joab assumes as a matter of course that a loyal soldier ought to have seized so golden an opportunity, and he is genuinely puzzled that the man let it pass. In that assumption the general's whole character stands disclosed: to Joab the safety of the state and the destruction of the rebel outweigh any charge a doting father-king might lay upon the army. Henry, weighing the exchange, observes that Joab 'chides the man for not dispatching him,' and that the reward he names shows how highly he prized the blow. Yet the soldier's silence-to-come and his coming reply will put Joab in the wrong on his own ground, for the man will appeal to the very charge Joab is here brushing aside. The verse is thus the pivot on which the tragedy turns: the king said 'deal gently,' and his chief captain, hearing that a subordinate has obeyed, treats the obedience as a fault to be corrected with silver and a soldier's belt.
12:
The soldier's answer to Joab is preserved by the witnesses as a rare portrait of integrity in a corrupt hour, and though the source here is thin — the reformed Gill alone comments directly, marking that the man spoke 'disdaining his proposal' — the verse rewards a closer reading of its own words and its setting. The man had just been offered silver and a soldier's belt for a single stroke, and his reply, running on into the verses that follow, begins by refusing the bribe outright: though he should receive a thousand shekels of silver in his hand, he would not put forth his hand against the king's son.

Gill's single word, 'disdaining,' is exact. The soldier does not weigh the offer and decline it reluctantly; he scorns it. Multiply Joab's ten shekels a hundredfold, and the answer would still be no. What makes the refusal weighty is the ground the man gives for it: 'for in our hearing the king charged thee and Abishai and Ittai, saying, Beware that none touch the young man Absalom.' The very charge Joab had brushed aside in v.11 is the charge the common soldier holds sacred. He had stood in the ranks when David, at the gate, gave his commanders their orders in the hearing of all the people (v.5), and he will not, for any reward, do what his king forbade before the whole army.

The contrast the witnesses let stand is stark and deliberate. Joab, entrusted with the charge by name, treats it as an obstacle to be paid around; the nameless man, who heard the charge only as one of the crowd, treats it as binding on his conscience. The soldier fears the king's displeasure more than he covets Joab's gold, and he fears, too, the guilt of the deed itself. His disdain is not merely prudence — though he knows, as the next verse will say, that nothing is hid from the king — but a genuine reverence for the word of his sovereign and for the life of the king's son.

Set within the chapter's larger frame, the verse quietly indicts the great and honours the small. The whole tragedy of Absalom's death turns on whether David's charge will be kept, and here, at the decisive moment, it is an unnamed foot-soldier, not the seasoned general, who keeps it. His refusal throws Joab's coming violence into sharp relief: the man who could have struck for a reward would not, while the man who was charged to spare will strike without one. In the soldier's plain 'no' the witnesses find the conscience of the loyal army speaking — the same loyalty that let Absalom pass unharmed in v.9 — and they let his obedience stand as the measure by which Joab's disobedience is judged.
13:
The soldier completes his refusal with a shrewd word about the king's all-seeing knowledge, and the witnesses find in the verse both a small textual crux and a large exposure of Joab's character. The grammatical-historical commentators open the textual question. Charles Ellicott notes that the English, like the Vulgate, follows the margin of the Hebrew — 'against mine own life' — while the Septuagint, following the text, reads 'against his life,' meaning Absalom's; 'either makes a good sense,' he judges, 'but the English is preferable.' John Gill, the reformed voice, records the same double reading: the marginal 'mine own life' makes the man say he would have exposed himself and acted falsely against his own conscience, while the textual 'his life' points to Absalom's life, taken by the blow. Keil and Delitzsch let the man's whole point stand: 'there is no matter hid from the king, and thou thyself wouldest have set thyself against me.'

That last clause is where the witnesses see Joab unmasked. The soldier knows his commander. Had he struck Absalom, the deed could not have been kept from David — nothing is hid from the king — and then Joab himself, for all his present urging, 'wouldest have set thyself against me.' Adam Clarke calls it 'a strong appeal to Joab's loyalty, and respect for the orders of David,' but adds that Joab 'was proof against every fine feeling, and against every generous sentiment.' Ellicott presses the same verdict: 'in this parley Joab thoroughly exposes his unscrupulous and self-willed character, and the man shows that he understood it.'

The puritan-evangelical Henry crystallizes the soldier's insight into a proverb: 'Those that love the treason hate the traitor.' Joab would gladly have profited by the murder, yet would have been the first to prosecute the murderer once the deed drew the king's wrath. The soldier sees the trap and steps clear of it. He will not be made the instrument of a killing that his master forbade and would afterward avenge, whatever bounty the general dangles.

Beneath the byplay the witnesses agree on what the verse teaches. The soldier's caution is not cowardice but wisdom joined to conscience: he reckons rightly both the king's justice and Joab's treachery, and he refuses to 'work falsehood' — against his own life, or against Absalom's, or against the plain command he had heard. Henry notes that Joab 'could not deny this, nor blame the man for his caution, and therefore makes him no answer, but breaks off the discourse.' The general is silenced by the soldier's honesty, and his only reply will be to take the deed into his own hands. The verse thus completes the portrait begun at the oak: the ordinary man holds the king's word and the king's justice in view together, while Joab, having no answer, turns from argument to the darts.
14:
Joab breaks off the parley and thrusts three weapons into Absalom, and here the witnesses hold their fullest debate: was the killing a justified act of state or plain murder? The grammatical-historical commentators frame the deed precisely. Charles Ellicott observes that the word rendered 'darts' means rather 'a rod or staff,' and that 'heart' is the same word as the following 'midst,' so the phrase is not to be pressed too literally: Joab 'seized such sticks as were at hand in the wood and thrust them into Absalom, giving him most painful and probably mortal wounds, but not instantly killing him.' Keil and Delitzsch agree that he caught up in his hurry the first thing he found, 'namely pointed staffs,' rendering Joab's curt refusal, 'Not so will I wait before thee' — I will not leave the thing to thee.

On the morality the witnesses are strikingly of one mind that the act was, in strict law, murder, even while they weigh what may be said for it. Adam Clarke lays out both sides with unusual fullness. Joab 'should have obeyed the king's commandment; and yet the safety of the state required the sacrifice of Absalom.' Independently of that, Clarke argues, Absalom's life was 'quadruply forfeited to the law' — for the murder of his brother Amnon; for exciting insurrection; for taking up arms against his own father (Deuteronomy 21:18); and for lying with his father's concubines (Leviticus 18:29) — so that long ago he should have died by the hand of justice. 'Yet, in the present circumstances,' Clarke concludes, 'Joab's act was base and disloyal, and a cowardly murder.' Ellicott reaches the same balance: defensible 'in a lawless age' as the one deed that ended the rebellion and made its renewal impossible, but 'a distinct disobedience of express orders,' and so 'wilful and deliberate murder.'

The puritan-evangelical Henry feels the contradiction most keenly. Joab threw the darts into Absalom's body 'which put him, no doubt, to exquisite torment, while he is yet alive in the midst of the oak. Was this to deal gently with the young man? Would David have suffered him to do it if he had been upon the spot?' Yet Henry allows the plea of the state: while Joab 'broke the order of a too indulgent father, he did real service both to his king and country,' and cites the maxim Salus populi suprema lex — the safety of the people is the supreme law. The reformed Gill records Joab's own resolve to do the deed himself rather than wait on the reluctant soldier.

The debate is genuine and unresolved: every witness grants both that Absalom's death served the kingdom and that Joab's hand in it was murder. What none of them will do is call the disobedience obedience. The general's private justice, however useful, tramples the king's public charge, and the witnesses leave the two verdicts standing together, unreconciled, exactly as the text leaves them.
15:
Ten of Joab's armour-bearers close round Absalom and finish the killing, and the witnesses read the detail as both a grim completion and a shared guilt. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record it plainly: 'ten young men that bare Joab's armour compassed about and smote Absalom, and slew him,' and they carry the sequence straight on to Joab's blowing of the trumpet to stay the pursuit. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley explains why the second blow was needed: 'the darts did not dispatch him, and therefore they smote him again, and killed him.' Joab's three staffs had wounded but not slain; the young men complete what their master began.

The reformed Gill identifies the ten as those 'that waited upon him in the battle, to carry his armour, and supply him with it as he should have occasion,' who now, 'by his orders,' surround and slay the helpless prince. The point is not incidental. By drawing his own attendants into the deed, Joab spreads the responsibility across a company and binds them to him; the killing of Absalom becomes the act of Joab's household troops, done at his command, so that no single hand — and every hand — is guilty.

The puritan-evangelical Henry supplies the scene's terrible pageantry. The young men 'surrounded him, made a ring about him in triumph, and then smote him and slew him' — a circle of executioners closing on a man who could neither fight nor fly, hanging exposed in the oak. Henry cannot watch it without turning to prayer against all who would overthrow the Lord's anointed: 'So let all thy enemies perish, O Lord!' The rebellion that had convulsed the kingdom dies here, in the wood, at the hands of ten armour-bearers.

The witnesses do not divide over the fact, but the shadow of the earlier debate lies across it. What Joab began in v.14 in flat defiance of the king's charge, his men now consummate; the 'deal gently' of v.5 is answered by a ring of spears. Yet the same witnesses who called Joab's stroke murder acknowledge, with Henry, that the death was the end of the danger and the deliverance of the kingdom. The armour-bearers' blows are at once the seal of Joab's disobedience and the close of the war. Absalom, who had stolen the hearts of Israel and driven his father from Jerusalem, dies surrounded and unpitied, and with him dies the cause that had gathered twenty thousand to their graves. The trumpet that Joab is about to sound will call the loyal army back from a slaughter that no longer serves any purpose, for the man for whom it was waged now lies dead in the pit.
16:
Joab sounds the trumpet and recalls the army from the pursuit, and the witnesses read the act as the mingling of a hard general's mercy with the plain logic of a finished war. The reformed Gill notes simply that the trumpet was blown 'as the sign of a retreat' — the signal that halts the killing. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke draws out the reason: Joab 'knew that the rebellion was now extinguished by the death of Absalom; and was not willing that any farther slaughter should be made of the deluded people.' The word 'deluded' is exact to Clarke's mind: the common Israelites who had followed Absalom were not implacable enemies but a misled multitude, and with their leader dead there was no cause to cut them down.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the trumpet-blast among its parallels — the same instrument that stayed Joab's hand after the death of Asahel (2 Samuel 2:28) and that will end the revolt of Sheba (2 Samuel 20:22) — and states the principle: 'with the death of Absalom the rebellion was at an end, and Joab would stop further slaughter.' Keil and Delitzsch add the human motive behind the strategy: Joab 'spared the people,' that is, 'he wanted to spare them.'

The puritan-evangelical Henry gathers the theology of the moment. 'The danger is over, now that Absalom is slain; the people will soon return to their allegiance to David, and therefore no more blood shall be spilt.' No prisoners are taken to be tried as traitors and made examples; instead, 'let every man return to his tent; they are all the king's subjects, all his good subjects again.' The rebellion once ended, the rebels are no longer enemies but citizens to be reclaimed, and the trumpet that stops the pursuit is already an act of reconciliation.

The witnesses do not dispute; they observe together the irony that the same Joab whose disobedient hand had just slain the king's son now shows a soldier's mercy to the king's misled subjects. The two acts are of a piece with his character: ruthless toward the head of the revolt, coolly practical toward its body. There is no glory to be won by slaughtering a broken and repentant people, and much to be lost, for every Israelite killed weakened the kingdom David must soon govern again. So the trumpet calls the loyal troops back from the wood, and the civil war, having devoured its twenty thousand and its prince, is stopped by the very man who had refused to let it stop short of Absalom's death. Mercy and severity meet in a single blast of the horn.
17:
Absalom's body is flung into a great pit and buried under a cairn of stones while all Israel flees home, and the witnesses read the burial as a deliberate mark of shame and a fulfilment of the law's sentence on a rebellious son. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the parallels that give the heap its meaning. Keil and Delitzsch call it 'an ignominious monument, like those thrown up over Achan' (Joshua 7:26) 'and the king of Ai' (Joshua 8:29) — the stone-heap being, in Israel, the token of a death under curse. Adam Clarke broadens the custom: the great heap of stones 'was the method of burying heroes, and even traitors,' designed to perpetuate the memory of the event, whether good or bad, and he likens it to the ancient cairns and barrows of Britain, Ireland, and Scotland. Charles Ellicott glosses the closing phrase, 'every one to his tent,' as an idiom carried down from the life in the wilderness, meaning simply every man to his home.

The reformed Gill sets the grave in its place — 'in the wood of Ephraim, near to which the battle was fought, and into which Absalom fled, and where he was slain' — so that the rebel is buried on the very ground of his overthrow, not carried home in honour.

The puritan-evangelical Henry presses the burial into its sharpest theological point. They would not bring the body to David, 'for that circumstance would but have added to his grief,' nor preserve it for honourable burial, 'but threw it into the next pit with indignation.' The great heap of stones was raised 'to signify that he ought to have been stoned as a rebellious son' (Deuteronomy 21:21), the law's own penalty enacted upon his corpse; and Henry recalls the prophet's word about iniquity lying heavy 'upon his bones' (Ezekiel 32:27). Where now, he asks, 'is the beauty he had been so proud of? Where are his aspiring projects, and the castles he had built in the air? His thoughts perish, and he with them.' Travelers, Henry adds, long marked the spot, casting a stone upon the heap with a curse on rebellious Absalom and on all wicked children who rise against their parents.

The witnesses agree without contention. The pit and the piled stones are no accident of a hasty battlefield but a judgment written in the ground: the man who had reared a pillar to immortalize his name is covered by a rude cairn of infamy, and the tribes who had crowded to make him king scatter, every one to his tent, leaving him to the burial of a traitor. The next verse will set his vain monument beside this heap, and the contrast will preach its own sermon.
18:
The narrator pauses over the pillar Absalom had raised for himself, and the witnesses read the flashback as a study in vanity crossed by providence. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the place and the thing. Keil and Delitzsch identify the king's dale with the valley named from Abraham's meeting with Melchizedek (Genesis 14:17), reckoned by Josephus two stadia from Jerusalem — 'therefore not close to the Dead Sea' — and describe the monument as a memorial of stone whose exact form, column or obelisk or monolith, cannot now be determined. Charles Ellicott agrees that the king's dale is the valley of Shaveh (Genesis 14:17), and though he notes that writers differ, thinks it 'probable' the site lay near Jerusalem in the valley of the Kidron. Adam Clarke reports that a marble pillar bearing Absalom's name stood in Josephus's day, and that one is shown under that name still, 'but it is comparatively a modern structure'; he adds that the Hebrew for 'place' is literally 'Absalom's Hand.'

All the witnesses feel the pathos of the reason Absalom gives: 'I have no son to keep my name in remembrance.' Ellicott and Henry both point back to the earlier notice that Absalom had three sons (2 Samuel 14:27), so that the childlessness here implies, as Henry puts it, that 'God had taken them away by death; and justly is a rebellious son written childless.' The man who would not honour his father is left with no son to honour him.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley states the irony flatly: the pillar was reared 'to preserve his name; whereas it had been more for his honour if his name had been buried in perpetual oblivion.' The reformed Gill notes that Absalom 'contrived the following scheme to perpetuate his memory' — a self-devised plan, as Kimchi explains it, taken up in his own lifetime.

The puritan-evangelical Henry draws the whole meditation to its edge. 'What foolish insignificant projects do proud men fill their heads with!' Absalom could not be content in the obscurity of David's other sons, 'of whom nothing is recorded but their names,' but 'would be famous, and is therefore justly made for ever infamous.' Providence crosses his design at every point: he built a marble pillar for glory, and got 'a rude heap of stones' for a monument; he sought to keep his name in remembrance, and it is remembered only 'to his everlasting dishonour.' The witnesses let the two mounds of the chapter face each other — the pillar of a man's pride in the king's dale, and the cairn of his shame in the wood — and read in the pair the ancient sentence that 'those that exalt themselves shall be abased.'
19:
The scene shifts to the carrying of the news, and the witnesses read the opening of it as a study in Joab's rough discernment and Ahimaaz's eager love. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the terms: Ahimaaz the son of Zadok wanted to run and tell the king 'that Jehovah had procured the king justice out of the hand of his enemies,' but Joab, 'knowing how David would receive the tidings of the death of Absalom,' answered that Ahimaaz was 'no man of good tidings to-day' — he should carry news another day, not this, 'even because the king's son is dead.' The very victory that Ahimaaz would proclaim is poisoned, in Joab's eyes, by the death that must be told with it.

The reformed Gill notes that Ahimaaz spoke to Joab, having stayed with the army after he and Jonathan had earlier brought Hushai's message down to David — the same swift and trusted young priest reappearing at the decisive hour.

The puritan-evangelical Henry unfolds the human motives on both sides. Ahimaaz was 'very forward to be the messenger of these tidings, so transported was he with joy that this cloud was blown over.' Henry is careful about his motive: he desired the errand 'not so much in hope of a reward (he was above that) as that he might have the pleasure and satisfaction of bringing the king, whom he loved, this good news.' But Joab 'knew David better than Ahimaaz did,' and saw that 'the tidings of Absalom's death, which must conclude the story, would spoil the acceptableness of all the rest.' Loving Ahimaaz, Joab would not let him be the bearer of news that would meet with a father's anguish rather than a king's thanks.

The witnesses do not divide; they trace together the collision of two goods. Ahimaaz's zeal is generous — he wants to be the first to gladden the king he loves with word that the Lord has avenged him. Joab's refusal is, in its own hard way, kind — he would shield the young priest from the ingratitude that awaits whoever brings a bereaved father the news of his son's death. Both men reckon rightly what the king will feel; only Ahimaaz, in his gladness, has not yet weighed how the death will swallow up the victory. The scene quietly prepares the reader for David's lament: everyone around the king, even the blunt Joab, already senses that the good news of deliverance will be received as the worst of news, because the enemy who fell was the king's own son. The runner's love and the general's caution alike bend before that foreknown grief.
20:
Joab overrules Ahimaaz and sends the Cushite instead, and the witnesses read his refusal as at once a shrewd calculation and a real tenderness toward the young priest. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the words their force: 'Thou shalt not bear tidings this day, but thou shalt bear tidings another day: but this day thou shalt bear no tidings, because the king's son is dead,' and so Joab 'entrusted the Cushite with the duty of conveying to David the announcement of what had occurred.' Charles Ellicott sharpens the point that the very word for the errand is loaded: 'the word is used, with rare exceptions, of good tidings,' and Ahimaaz was in favour both with David and with Joab; therefore Joab, 'well knowing how painful to David would be the news of the death of Absalom, refused to let Ahimaaz bear it.'

The reformed Gill reads the same motive: 'Joab knew the tidings of Absalom's death would not be acceptable to the king; and Ahimaaz being a good man, and the son of a priest, for whom Joab had a respect, he would not send the tidings by him,' since such a message 'would not recommend him to the king.' The general spares his favourite the thanklessness of the office.

The puritan-evangelical Henry sees the prudence in it and the difference of persons: the tidings of Absalom's death 'are fitter to be brought by a footman than by a priest.' A slave in Joab's service can carry unwelcome news and risk the king's displeasure where a beloved young priest should not.

The witnesses agree, and behind their agreement lies a memory that gives Joab's caution its edge — the fate of earlier bearers of a king's death. Henry recalls, on the surrounding verses, the danger that a messenger of such news might suffer as those did who reported to David the deaths of Saul and of Ish-bosheth. Joab knows the peril of the office and knows, too, that Ahimaaz does not yet grasp how the victory will be swallowed in the father's grief. So he holds the young priest back and dispatches a man whose displeasure would cost less. The verse turns on a single, humane insight, shared by every witness: that the same word which means 'good news' will, in David's ear this day, mean the worst, because the enemy overthrown was the son he loved; and that it is mercy to keep a friend from being the one to speak it.
21:
Joab dispatches the Cushite to the king, and the witnesses spend their comment chiefly on the identity of this runner and on the secrecy of his errand. The reformed Gill canvasses who the man was: 'the Ethiopian, or blackamoor,' either an Ethiopian by birth and a proselyte, or an Israelite of dark complexion so named; he records that some Jewish writers identified him with Cush the Benjaminite of the seventh Psalm's title, and that some took him for one of the ten young men who had just slain Absalom — though Gill notes it 'would have been dangerous for one of these to have carried the tidings,' had David known it. Charles Ellicott, the grammatical-historical voice, reads the name as a common noun, 'the Cushite, probably an Ethiopian slave in Joab's service, for whose falling under the king's displeasure he had little care' — precisely the disposable messenger the previous verse required.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke fastens on a point of timing that will matter greatly at the runners' arrival: 'at this time the death of Absalom was not publicly known; but Joab had given Cushi private information of it. This Ahimaaz had not, for he could not tell the king whether Absalom were dead.' The Cushite alone carries the whole truth; Ahimaaz, should he run, will know only the victory. Clarke ties this to Joab's coming remark that Ahimaaz has 'no tidings ready.' Keil and Delitzsch add only that the Cushite 'bowed himself unto Joab, and ran' — a servant's silent obedience where Ahimaaz had pressed and argued.

The witnesses do not contend, but their notes converge on a quiet irony. The man chosen to bear the news is the one whose standing counts for least — a foreign slave, or an obscure soldier — precisely because the news is the kind that endangers its bearer. Joab has weighed the office and matched it to a man he can spare. Yet the same lowliness that makes the Cushite expendable also makes him the one entrusted with the full and painful truth, while the honoured young priest is kept, for the moment, in the dark. The verse thus completes the arrangement begun in v.20: the beloved runner held back, the disposable runner sent forward, and the death of the king's son committed to lips whose grief or favour the king will scarcely regard. Around this small calculation of persons the drama of the double message is set, and the stage is prepared for Ahimaaz's stubborn second request.
22:
Ahimaaz will not be denied, and begs a second time to run after the Cushite; the witnesses read his persistence and Joab's reluctant leave, and puzzle a little over the difficult phrase Joab uses. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the young priest's plea, 'But howsoever, let me, I pray thee, also run after Cushi' — 'let whatever will happen' — and note that though Joab expostulated repeatedly, Ahimaaz could not be induced to give up his purpose, so that at length Joab permitted him, and he ran so fast that he outstripped the Cushite. Joab's demurral, 'Wherefore wilt thou run, my son, seeing that thou hast no tidings ready?' they gloss as meaning there was 'no striking message' for him, no news that would strike the mark or effect anything.

Charles Ellicott takes up the same crux, 'no tidings ready,' and confesses it 'a difficult one.' He records the ancient attempts — the Septuagint's 'no tidings leading to profit,' the Vulgate's 'thou wilt not be a bearer of good tidings' — and offers as 'the simplest and most probable sense' that Ahimaaz had 'no tidings sufficient' to warrant a special messenger, since the Cushite had already carried the news. The reformed Gill marks only the young man's temper: 'he could not be easy, even though a messenger was dispatched, but pressed Joab still.'

The witnesses do not divide on the substance, and Henry, from his overview, supplies the motive the terse text withholds. Why should Ahimaaz be so fond of an office already given to another? Henry offers two readings, and holds both charitably. Perhaps it was to show his swiftness: observing how heavily the Cushite ran, and by what a poor route, Ahimaaz 'had a mind to show how fast he could run,' and perhaps 'was proud of it' — though Henry adds dryly that swiftness of foot is 'no great praise for a priest.' Or perhaps, more kindly, it was 'in prudence and tenderness to the king': knowing he could get before the Cushite, Ahimaaz wished to prepare David 'by a vague and general report' for the plain truth the slower runner would tell, on the principle that 'if bad news must come, it is best that it come gradually, and will be the better borne.'

Between these two motives the witnesses do not finally decide, and the ambiguity is fitting, for Ahimaaz's conduct at the gate will bear out both — his eagerness to be first, and his careful holding back of the one fact that would wound. What all agree is that Joab, having warned him he carried no message worth the running, at last indulges the young man's importunity, and Ahimaaz speeds away by a smoother road to reach the king before the bearer of the truth.
23:
Ahimaaz gets his leave, runs by 'the way of the plain,' and overtakes the Cushite, and the witnesses use the geographical note to settle both how he could outrun the man already sent and where the battle must have lain. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the fullest treatment. The word rendered 'plain' is generally applied to the valley of the Jordan, and some have argued from it that the battle could not have been on the eastern side of the river, since Ahimaaz could then have reached Mahanaim by the Jordan valley 'only by a long and tedious detour.' But Ellicott answers that the word 'simply means circuit, or surrounding country,' as it does of the district about Jerusalem (Nehemiah 12:28); here it signifies that Ahimaaz ran 'by the way of the circuit' — 'in all probability, by a longer but smoother road than that taken by the Cushite, so that he was able to outrun him.' The seeming detour becomes the key to the race: the fleeter runner takes the easier ground and beats the man who went the shorter, rougher way.

The reformed Gill notes only the granting of the request — 'being so very importunate, it was granted' — and lets the drama pass to the walls of the city. Keil and Delitzsch carry the narrative to the watchman: David 'sat between the two gates of Mahanaim waiting for tidings of the result of the battle,' in the small court between the outer and inner gate, while the watchman went up to the roof of the gate and, lifting his eyes, saw a man running alone.

The witnesses do not divide; they cooperate to remove an apparent difficulty. The two runners are the chapter's small parable of message-bearing: the Cushite, sent first with the whole truth, takes the direct but harder path; Ahimaaz, sent second and knowing less, takes the roundabout but smoother one and arrives first. Ellicott's philology quietly defends the coherence of the account against those who would use the word 'plain' to relocate the battle — the same battlefield-geography he had argued at v.6 — and shows that the text hangs together once the local terms are rightly understood. Behind the footrace stands the king, seated in the gate, straining after news of a victory whose central fact, the death of his son, will turn all its gladness to grief. The scene is set for the double arrival, and the witnesses have cleared the ground of every objection but the one that matters — the father's dread of what the runners bring.
24:
David sits between the two gates while the watchman climbs to the roof and spies a lone runner, and the witnesses attend to the architecture of the scene and the suspense it holds. The reformed Gill explains the setting: Mahanaim, being a fortified place, 'had two walls, one within another, and in each wall a gate; and between these David sat, waiting for news of the battle.' The wesleyan-arminian commentators fill in the picture. Adam Clarke supposes David 'in the seat of justice,' seated in the space between an outer and an inner door, with 'a turret, on which a sentinel or watchman stood continually, and gave information of what he saw in the country.' John Wesley notes that the gates of those cities were 'large and thick; and for the greater security, had two gates, one more outward, the other inward,' and that David sat there 'that he might hear tidings when any came into the city.' Keil and Delitzsch agree that the two gates are the outer and inner gate of the city wall, with a small court between where the king waited.

The witnesses do not debate; they build up, detail by detail, the tense stillness of a king who cannot bear to be far from the news. David has been kept from the battle by his people's insistence (v.3), and now the enforced idleness of the reserve tells on him: he sits in the gate, the place of counsel and of judgment, but his mind is not on judgment. The watchman on the tower is his eyes, and the single figure running alone in the distance is the first thread of intelligence from a field whose outcome will decide his throne and his heart at once.

Matthew Henry, surveying the passage, catches the pathos of the arrangement. David 'stayed behind at the city of Mahanaim, some miles from the wood where the battle was, and in the utmost border of the land,' and because Absalom's scattered forces fled homeward the contrary way, the watchman could learn nothing of the issue until an express came on purpose. So the king 'sat in the gate expecting to hear.' The witnesses leave him there, poised between hope and fear, a monarch whose greatest anxiety is not for the crown but for the son who wore arms against him. The lone runner the watchman sees is the sign, as the next verse will say, of tidings rather than of rout; but the tidings themselves the king will scarcely be able to receive, for his one question, already forming, is not 'Have we won?' but 'Is the young man safe?'
25:
The watchman cries out, and David reads the solitary runner as a sign of news rather than of defeat, and the witnesses find in the king's inference a shrewd point of ancient warfare. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the reasoning: 'If he comes alone, there is good news in his mouth,' namely 'because several runners would have shown themselves if it had been a flight.' A single man speeding toward the city means a messenger dispatched with word; a beaten army would have sent not one runner but a scattered crowd of fugitives streaming back in disorder. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke states the same principle plainly: 'If he be alone, there is tidings — that is, good tidings. For if the battle had been lost men would have been running in different directions through the country.'

The reformed Gill marks only the mechanics of the moment — the watchman 'called with a loud voice from the roof of the watchtower to the king, sitting between the gates, and informed him what he saw.' Keil and Delitzsch carry the scene forward: as the first runner came nearer, the watchman saw a second man running, and shouted it down into the gate, whereupon the king said, 'This is also a good messenger.'

The witnesses do not divide; they observe together how the king's practised eye reads the field from a single figure. David has commanded armies all his life, and he knows the look of victory and the look of rout: victory sends one man ahead with word, rout sends a hundred back in panic. His confidence that the news is good is well founded, and it heightens the tragedy to come, for the very soundness of his military judgment cannot prepare his heart for the one item of the good news that will undo him. He infers rightly that the battle is won; he cannot yet know, and dares to hope against, what the winning cost. The lone runner is indeed the herald of a triumph, and the king reads him truly — yet the triumph he heralds carries within it the death of the king's son. So the witnesses let the moment stand as a small masterpiece of dramatic irony: the king rejoices in the sign of good tidings, while the reader, and Joab, and the runners themselves, already know that the tidings will break him. The second runner the watchman now spies will only confirm the victory and, at length, disclose the grief.
26:
The watchman spies a second runner, and the king takes him too for a bearer of news, and the witnesses read the growing suspense as the report draws toward its bitter core. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch narrate the moment: the watchman 'called unto the porter, and said, Behold another man running alone,' and the king replied, 'He also bringeth tidings' — reading the second lone runner, like the first, as a herald rather than a fugitive. Then, as the foremost drew nearer, the watchman ventured a recognition: 'Me thinketh the running of the foremost is like the running of Ahimaaz the son of Zadok,' to which David answered, 'He is a good man, and cometh with good tidings.' The reformed Gill marks the plain progress of it — the watchman 'saw another man running,' at a further distance behind the first.

The witnesses do not divide, and the interest of the verse lies in the king's confident inference from the runner's identity. David reasons from the man to the message: because Ahimaaz is 'a good man,' the news he carries must be good. Keil and Delitzsch supply the further ground — Joab 'would not have selected him to bring any other than good news.' The logic is sound as far as it goes, yet the reader knows what David does not: that Ahimaaz has been sent against Joab's judgment, carrying only the victory and not the whole truth, so that the king's very confidence in the messenger will draw him deeper into a hope that the second runner is charged to end.

Matthew Henry, from his overview, catches the poignancy of the king's trust. Ahimaaz was so famed for his running that he was known by it at a distance, 'and so eminently good that it is taken for granted, if he be the messenger, the news must needs be good.' Henry turns it to a wish for the gospel's own messengers: 'It is pity but the good tidings of the gospel should always be brought by good men; and how welcome should the messengers be to us for their message sake!' The verse thus holds the king a moment longer in the light before the shadow: he sees a trusted friend running fast and true toward the gate, and reads in him the promise of joy, while the second figure laboring behind carries the sorrow that the first will not speak. The witnesses leave the two runners closing on the city, and the king's heart rising to meet a gladness it will not be allowed to keep.
27:
The watchman names the foremost runner as Ahimaaz, and David welcomes the recognition as a pledge of good news, and the witnesses read the exchange for the king's confidence and the young priest's repute. The reformed Gill glosses the watchman's idiom, 'me thinketh,' as 'I see, I perceive; so it appears to me' — the sentinel judging by the manner of the running before the man's face can be made out. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain how the recognition was possible: the watchman knew Ahimaaz 'by the swiftness of his running,' and when he announced it the king replied, 'He is a good man, and cometh with good tidings,' reasoning that Joab 'would not have selected him to bring any other than good news.'

The witnesses do not divide, and Matthew Henry supplies the fuller portrait. Ahimaaz 'was so famous for running that he was known by it at a distance,' and 'so eminently good' a man that the king takes it for granted his message must be good: 'He is a good man, zealously affected to the king's interest, and would not bring bad news.' Henry lifts the moment into a wish for the ministers of the gospel — that its 'good tidings' should 'always be brought by good men,' and that the messengers might be welcome 'for their message sake.'

Yet the very grounds of David's confidence are what the reader knows to be misplaced, and the witnesses let the irony stand. The king argues from three true premises — that a lone runner means news, that this runner is the swift and faithful Ahimaaz, and that Joab would not send so good a man with bad news — to a conclusion that is about to fail him. For Ahimaaz, though good and swift and sent, carries only half the story; the death of the king's son, which Joab kept from him, is the one thing his good tidings will not contain, and the king will have to wait for the labouring Cushite to learn it. The chapter thus turns the king's soundest reasoning against his deepest hope. He is right that the battle is won and right that Ahimaaz is a good man; he is wrong only in supposing that good news and a good messenger can spare him the grief that is running, more slowly, close behind. The witnesses hold David a last moment in his confidence before the runner's arrival begins to unravel it.
28:
Ahimaaz arrives, cries 'Peace,' falls on his face, and blesses God for the victory, and the witnesses read his greeting for its piety and for the careful thing it leaves unsaid. The grammatical-historical commentators render the salute. Keil and Delitzsch note that Ahimaaz 'called out to the king, Shalom,' that is, 'Hail!' or 'All is well,' then 'fell down before him to greet him reverentially' and said, 'Blessed be Jehovah thy God, who hath given up the men that lifted up their hand against my lord the king.' Charles Ellicott hears in the single word 'Peace' the cry 'with which Ahimaaz greets the king in his eager haste, as soon as he comes within hearing,' before he approaches and falls down 'with a distinct announcement of the victory.'

The puritan-evangelical Henry dwells on the godliness of the report. Ahimaaz proclaims the triumph — 'they are all cut off that lifted up their hands against the king' — but, 'as became a priest, while he gives the king the joy of it, he gives God the glory of it, the God of peace and war, the God of salvation and victory.' His 'Blessed be the Lord thy God, that has done this for thee' Henry reads as a deliberate turning of David's heart upward, pursuant to the promise to uphold his throne (2 Samuel 7:16); and he sees a pastoral wisdom in it: 'By directing David thus to give God thanks for his victory, he prepared him for the approaching news of its allay.' The more the heart is enlarged in thanksgiving for mercies, Henry adds, 'the better disposed we shall be to bear with patience the afflictions mixed with them.'

The witnesses do not divide, but they mark, each in his way, the significant silence in Ahimaaz's speech. He announces the deliverance in full and the death not at all — the reverent bow, the blessing of God, the report that the king's enemies are overthrown, and then nothing of the one enemy whose fall the king most dreads. The reformed Gill notes only that he 'said with a loud voice' as soon as he came within earshot, the eager herald of good news. What every witness feels is the tension between the young priest's genuine devotion and the incompleteness of his message: his praise is sincere, his theology sound, his joy real, yet he has framed the whole so as to hold back the fact that Joab would not let him carry. The blessing of God is true and timely, and it is also, as Henry sees, a merciful preparation — the king lifted first to thanksgiving, that he may be the better able to bear the blow the next moment will bring.
29:
David's one question — 'Is the young man Absalom safe?' — meets Ahimaaz's evasion, and the witnesses divide over whether the young priest spoke from ignorance or from a tender untruth. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke lays out both readings. On the one hand, 'it is very probable that Ahimaaz did not know of the death of Absalom; he had seen the rout of his army, but did not know of his death.' On the other, 'others think he knew all, and told this untruth that he might not be the messenger of bad news to David.' Clarke leaves the two open. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch incline to the first: Ahimaaz 'spoke as if he had been sent off before Absalom's fate had been decided or could be known,' pointing to the Cushite — 'the king's servant' — just then approaching as the man who would know. But Charles Ellicott presses the harder verdict: Ahimaaz 'skilfully, though untruthfully, evades the question,' and adds the mordant note that 'he had just been trained to untruthfulness in David's service' — a glance back at the stratagems of the rebellion, in which Ahimaaz and Jonathan had played their part.

The reformed Gill turns from the messenger to the king, and finds in David's very question the measure of his heart: 'David seemed more concerned for him than for his army and the success of it; and even suggests as if it was nothing if Absalom was not safe, so great were his affections towards him.' The victory is announced, the enemies overthrown, and the king asks after one man only.

The puritan-evangelical Henry harmonizes the accounts by attributing to Ahimaaz a merciful discretion. The young priest 'soon discerned, what Joab intimated to him, that the death of the king's son would make the tidings of the day very unwelcome, and therefore in his report left that matter doubtful'; though he gave occasion to suspect how it was, he referred the king to the next messenger, 'that the thunderclap might not come too suddenly upon the poor perplexed king.'

The debate is genuine and the witnesses do not force it shut: whether Ahimaaz honestly did not know, or knew and gently dissembled, the evasion serves the same end — it spares David the blow a moment longer and shifts the burden to the Cushite. What all agree, with Gill, is that the king's question has already betrayed him. Poor David, as Henry says elsewhere, 'is so much a father that he forgets he is a king,' and cannot rejoice in a victory until he knows whether the young man is safe. The evasion answers his dread by deferring it, and turns him, as he bids Ahimaaz stand aside, to wait for the runner who will not spare him.
30:
David bids Ahimaaz stand aside to await the second runner, and the witnesses, though brief, read the command as the deliberate act of a king who means to weigh his news. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the plain purpose: the king said, 'Turn, and stand here,' 'that he might hear the further news from the Cushite, who had just arrived.' The wesleyan-arminian Clarke sharpens the motive into method: David 'intended to confront two messengers, and compare their accounts.' The king, unsatisfied by Ahimaaz's doubtful answer, will set the two reports side by side and draw the truth from their agreement or their difference. The reformed Gill reads it the same way — David had Ahimaaz 'turn aside, and stand' near him, 'until the other messenger came, that he might learn from them both the true state of the case.'

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse, slight as it is, discloses the king's frame of mind. David has heard the victory proclaimed and the death left in shadow, and he is not content. He does not dismiss the ambiguity, nor does he seize on Ahimaaz's evasion as comfort; he holds the young priest by him and waits for corroboration. It is the instinct of a man who has governed long and knows how news is shaped by the men who bring it — the same king who inferred rightly from a lone runner that the battle was won now declines to be satisfied by a single, hedging report about the one life he cares for.

Yet the very carefulness that makes David a wise reader of messengers is here the instrument of his own wounding. By setting Ahimaaz aside to wait for the Cushite, he ensures that he will hear the truth plainly and soon; the deferral Ahimaaz had won for him is short-lived, for the king himself calls for the fuller account. Henry, surveying the scene, notes that Ahimaaz, having delivered his message, 'refers him to the next messenger, whom they saw coming, for a more particular account of it,' and is bidden to stand by till the Cushite comes. So the king's prudence and the young priest's tenderness both give way to the plain report now approaching. David will have his comparison of accounts, and the comparison will spare him nothing. The witnesses leave him waiting between the two runners — the one who softened the news and the one who will not — poised on the edge of the grief the whole chapter has been moving toward.
31:
The Cushite arrives with his blunt announcement of victory, and the witnesses read his coming as the plainer counterpart to Ahimaaz's careful greeting. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render his words: 'Let my lord the king receive good tidings, for Jehovah hath procured thee justice to-day out of the hand of all who have risen up against thee' — the very theme, they note, that Ahimaaz had wished to carry in v.19, now spoken by the man Joab actually sent. The reformed Gill marks only that the Cushite 'came a little after' the swifter Ahimaaz, the slower but surer messenger arriving in his turn.

The witnesses do not divide, and the interest of the verse lies in the likeness and the difference between the two reports. Both runners open with the language of thanksgiving — the Lord has avenged the king of his enemies — and so both confirm the victory beyond doubt. But where Ahimaaz had prefaced his news with a reverent 'Peace' and a blessing of God, then fallen silent on the death, the Cushite comes straight to the announcement of triumph and will, at the king's question, come just as straight to the truth about Absalom. He is the disposable messenger of v.21, the man whose displeasure Joab did not weigh, and he carries the whole of what he knows.

The doubling of the good news serves the king's own design, for David had set Ahimaaz aside precisely to hear the further account (v.30) and compare the two. On the victory the reports agree, and the agreement is complete: the rebellion is crushed, and the God who promised to establish David's throne has vindicated him against all who rose up. Yet the very completeness of the good news only sets in sharper relief the one question that still hangs unanswered. The king has heard twice over that his enemies are overthrown; he has not yet heard whether, among those enemies, his son is numbered with the slain. The Cushite's opening words give him all the comfort a victory can give and none of the answer his heart demands. The witnesses leave the king poised for his second asking — the same question he put to Ahimaaz — and the reader knows that this messenger, unlike the first, will not turn it aside.
32:
David puts to the Cushite the same question he had put to Ahimaaz, and the messenger answers with a courteous circumlocution that yet leaves no doubt, and the witnesses read the veiled reply as the moment the truth breaks through. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch give the Cushite's words their weight: 'May it happen to the enemies of my lord the king, and all who have risen up against thee for evil, as to the young man' — and they observe that 'the death of Absalom was indicated clearly enough in these words.' Charles Ellicott marks the difference between the two messengers: the Cushite 'does not appreciate the state of the king's feelings, and answers with sufficient plainness, though in courteous phrase, that Absalom is dead.' Where Ahimaaz had evaded, the Cushite, caring less for the king's heart, tells him.
