The reformed Gill notes that David asks 'the same question that was put to Ahimaaz,' which 'shows what lay nearest his heart, and was uppermost in his mind.' Twice now the king has passed by the whole news of the victory to ask after one life. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke calls this fixation 'the utmost of his solicitude,' and judges that it 'well merited the reproof which Joab gave him' afterward (2 Samuel 19:5) — the general's blunt rebuke that David loved his enemies and hated his friends.

The witnesses do not divide over the sense of the Cushite's phrase; they admire its terrible tact. The messenger does not say outright that Absalom is hanged and pierced and buried under a heap of stones; he wraps the fact in a wish — that all the king's enemies may fare as that young man has fared. It is at once a form of loyalty, ranking Absalom among the traitors, and a way of speaking the unspeakable without the brutal word. Yet the courtesy cannot soften what it conveys. The king, who has been reading messengers all evening with a practised eye, cannot fail to read this: to bid that the king's foes be 'as that young man is' is to say plainly that the young man is dead. The whole chapter's suspense collapses into this single, decorous sentence. The victory is complete, the rebellion is ended, the Lord has avenged his anointed — and the one thing the father dreaded above every mercy has come to pass. The witnesses leave the king with the truth now fully in his hand, and turn, in the last verse, to watch it break him.
33:
The king breaks, and climbs weeping to the chamber over the gate crying 'O my son Absalom,' and the witnesses gather at this most famous of laments to weigh a father's love, a king's failure, and a possible glimpse of the Cross. The grammatical-historical commentators register the depth of the anguish. Keil and Delitzsch describe him agitated, going up to the balcony of the gate and pacing as he wept, 'My son Absalom, my son, my son Absalom! Oh that I had died for thee'; and they name the two forces in it — 'the excessive tenderness, or rather weakness, of David's paternal affection,' and his 'anger that Joab and his generals should have paid so little regard to his command to deal gently with Absalom' — which together 'entirely prevented him from taking a just and correct view of the crime of his rebel son, which merited death, and of the penal justice of God which had been manifested in his destruction.' Charles Ellicott finds the grief deepened 'from the thought — which must often have come to him during the progress of this rebellion — that all this sin and wrong took its occasion from his own great sin'; yet he judges David 'criminally weak at this crisis in allowing the feelings of the father completely to outweigh the duties of the monarch.'

The puritan-evangelical Henry presses the rebuke hardest and then lifts it highest. David is to be blamed, he writes, for so fond a love of a graceless son, for 'quarrelling with divine justice,' for 'opposing the justice of the nation,' and for 'despising the mercy of his deliverance'; the best of men 'are not always in an equally good frame,' and 'what we over-loved we are apt to over-grieve for.' Yet Henry ends by seeing in the weeping king 'a shadow of the Saviour's love, who wept over, prayed for, and even suffered death for mankind, though vile rebels and enemies.'

The witnesses divide gently over the wish 'would God I had died for thee.' The wesleyan-arminian Clarke will not close the door of hope: he wonders whether Absalom, hanging in the tree, 'implored the mercy of his Maker,' and whether the very suspension was 'a respite' granted him to deprecate the wrath of divine justice — 'humanity will delight in such a case to lay hold even on possibilities.' Wesley weighs the same reading and inclines the other way: the cry might spring 'from a deep sense of his eternal state, because he died in his sins,' but 'it seems rather to be the effect of strong passion, causing him to speak unadvisedly with his lips.' Henry, too, wishes he 'could see reason to think' the grief arose from concern for Absalom's soul, but judges it 'spoken inconsiderately, and in a passion.' The debate is tender and unresolved — whether David mourned a lost soul or only indulged a father's weakness — and over it all the witnesses set Henry's closing turn, that in this broken king weeping for a rebel son we catch, however dimly, the love of the true King who died for his enemies.
